scientificName: Hymenochirus boettgeri — AMPHIBIAN (order Anura, family Pipidae), not a fish, but commonly kept in freshwater aquariums; filed under taxon=fish as a pragmatic concession pending schema amphibian support

nativeRange.habitat: Shallow, slow-moving or still freshwater pools, forest streams, flooded fields, and ponds with dense leaf litter and aquatic vegetation; typically in warm lowland tropical forest

decorPreferences: dense planting (Java fern, Java moss, hornwort, Amazon sword) for hiding cover
leaf litter or driftwood to replicate forest-floor biotope
shallow caves, PVC tubes, or coconut huts for shelter
open sandy areas on the substrate for resting
low-flow filtration (sponge filter preferred) — strong currents stress ADFs

diet.notes: Carnivore; feeds primarily on meaty live and frozen foods. Preferred foods: frozen bloodworms, frozen brine shrimp, frozen daphnia, frozen mysis shrimp, and blackworms. Will rarely accept dry foods; sinking pellets formulated for amphibians (e.g., Hikari Tropical Fancy Guppy or dedicated frog pellets) may be accepted by trained individuals. ADFs have poor eyesight and slow reaction times — target feeding with tweezers or a pipette is strongly recommended to ensure they outcompete faster fish tankmates. They locate food by smell and water disturbance rather than sight.

goodWith: other African dwarf frogs (best kept in groups of 2+)
small peaceful nano fish (ember tetras, chili rasboras, celestial pearl danios)
avoidWith: African clawed frog (Xenopus laevis) — much larger and will predate ADFs
aggressive or nippy fish (tiger barbs, cichlids, bettas — may fin-nip ADFs)
large or predatory fish that will consume ADFs
small shrimp (dwarf shrimp, neocaridina) — ADFs will prey on them
very small fry — will be eaten
fast-feeding fish that outcompete ADFs for food

imageCandidates.notes: Clear lateral view of adult Hymenochirus boettgeri showing characteristic small size and webbed feet.

summary: Tiny, fully aquatic amphibians native to Central/West African forest pools. Peaceful and social, best kept in groups in well-planted nano tanks. Must have unobstructed surface access to breathe air. Not to be confused with the larger, predatory African Clawed Frog (Xenopus laevis).
careNotes:
The African Dwarf Frog is a fully aquatic amphibian that spends its entire life underwater, surfacing briefly every 10–20 minutes to gulp air — a behaviour called obligate aerial respiration. Tank lids are essential; ADFs are surprisingly capable escape artists and will perish rapidly out of water. Water surface must be unobstructed and within easy swimming reach (tank height under ~30 cm is preferable). Keep in groups of two or more; lone ADFs frequently waste away from stress.

Filtration must be gentle. A sponge filter is ideal; powerful powerheads or canister intakes should be covered with a pre-filter sponge to prevent small frogs from being sucked in. ADFs are extremely sensitive to copper — all copper-based medications and some algaecides must be avoided entirely; even trace copper can be lethal. Ammonia and nitrite spikes are equally dangerous given their permeable skin.

Feeding is the primary care challenge. ADFs have rudimentary eyesight and find food by detecting water movement and scent rather than visual tracking. In a community tank with faster fish they will almost always lose the competition for food. Target-feed with tweezers or a turkey baster/pipette: place frozen thawed bloodworms or brine shrimp directly in front of the frog 2–3 times per week. Signs of starvation (visible ribcage, flattened abdomen, “floaty” syndrome) develop within weeks if feeding is inadequate.

NOTE: The African Dwarf Frog (Hymenochirus boettgeri, family Pipidae) is frequently confused with the African Clawed Frog (Xenopus laevis). Key differences: ADFs have webbed front feet and grow to only 3–4 cm; Xenopus have clawed, unwebbed front feet and reach 10–13 cm. Xenopus are predatory, illegal to possess in some US states, and completely unsuitable for community tanks.
breedingNotes: Breeding in captivity is achievable with conditioning and a temperature drop trigger. A 2–3°C reduction in water temperature for 1–2 weeks followed by a gradual return to normal temperature, combined with abundant live/frozen food, stimulates spawning. Males produce a characteristic high-pitched singing/buzzing sound (an amplexus call) during courtship. The amplexus position is inguinal (the male clasps the female at the waist rather than behind the front legs). Eggs are laid at the surface during a dramatic “nuptial embrace spin,” then released singly to float or sink. The female may deposit 100–500 eggs per spawning event. Eggs hatch in 48–72 hours; tadpoles are filter-feeders and require infusoria or commercial tadpole/fry food. Metamorphosis occurs in approximately 4–6 weeks. Adults show no parental care and should be removed from the breeding tank to prevent egg predation.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op — African Dwarf Frog Care Guide
primary.notes: Primary trade husbandry source. Confirmed fully aquatic nature, obligate air-breathing, adult size 3–4 cm, temperature 22–28°C, pH 6.5–7.5, minimum tank 10 gal (38 L, rounded to 40 L), sponge filter recommendation, copper sensitivity, feeding strategy (target feeding), grouping (minimum 2), and Xenopus confusion warning. Supplemented with AmphibiaWeb and Practical Fishkeeping for taxonomy, range, and water chemistry detail.

additional.name: AmphibiaWeb — Hymenochirus boettgeri
additional.notes: Authoritative amphibian taxonomy and range source (substituted for FishBase which does not cover amphibians). Confirmed taxonomy: order Anura, family Pipidae, genus Hymenochirus. Native range: Nigeria, Cameroon, Gabon, DRC, CAR. Fully aquatic; obligate aerial respirator. Validated scientific name Hymenochirus boettgeri (Tornier, 1896).

additional.name: Practical Fishkeeping — African Dwarf Frog
additional.notes: UK aquarium trade source. Confirmed water parameters (temperature 24–26°C, soft slightly acidic water preferred), habitat (shallow forest pools and streams), diet (live/frozen meaty foods, target feeding essential), breeding behaviour (inguinal amplexus, surface spawning spin, 100–500 eggs, tadpoles filter-feed), and Xenopus identification guide. Lifespan 3–5 years cited.

additional.name: Reptiles Magazine — African Dwarf Frog Care Sheet
additional.notes: Specialist amphibian/reptile hobbyist source (substituted for SeriouslyFish which does not cover amphibians). Confirmed obligate aerial respiration (surfaces every 10–20 min), copper toxicity, escape risk (tight-fitting lids essential), feeding difficulty in community setups, and skin permeability sensitivity to water quality. Validated gH 5–15, kH 2–8 soft-water preference.

fish.breedingNotes: Breeding requires a temperature-drop trigger (2–3°C reduction for 1–2 weeks) followed by return to normal temp. Males sing/buzz during courtship; inguinal amplexus and dramatic nuptial spin at the surface. 100–500 floating eggs per spawn. Tadpoles filter-feed on infusoria; metamorphosis in 4–6 weeks. Remove adults post-spawn.
